questions.question: Explain the difference between ios::app and ios::ate modes in C++ file handling.
keyPoints: ios::app positions pointer at end before each write operation
ios::ate positions pointer at end only once when file opens
ios::app preserves existing data, ios::ate allows writing anywhere but starts at end

questions.question: Write a C++ program to copy contents from one text file to another.
keyPoints: Include fstream header
Open source file in read mode
Open destination in write mode
Use while loop with getline to read and write line by line
Close both files

questions.question: How do you check if a file was opened successfully? Mention two methods.
keyPoints: Using is_open() method returns bool
Using fail() method checks failbit
Checking if stream object evaluates to true in boolean context
Checking failbit after attempted open

questions.question: Explain the purpose of seekg() and seekp() functions in file handling.
keyPoints: seekg() positions the get pointer for reading operations
seekp() positions the put pointer for writing operations
Both take offset and reference position (ios::beg, ios::cur, ios::end)
Used for random access in files

questions.question: What is the difference between reading using >> operator and getline() function?
keyPoints: >> reads whitespace-separated tokens, stops at whitespace
getline() reads entire line including spaces until newline
>> leaves newline in buffer, getline() consumes newline
getline() is better for reading lines of text
>> can be used with primitive types directly

questions.question: Write the syntax to open a file using fstream for both reading and writing.
keyPoints: Include <fstream> header
Create fstream object: fstream file
Open with mode: file.open("filename.txt", ios::in | ios::out
Check if file opened successfully using is_open()
Close using file.close()

questions.question: Write a complete C++ program that creates a file, writes student information (name, roll number, marks) to it, reads the data back, and displays it. Include proper error handling.
keyPoints: Define student structure/class
Open file in write mode using ofstream
Accept student data from user
Write using << operator or write()
Close and reopen in read mode
Read using ifstream
Display data with proper formatting
Handle file open errors with is_open() or fail()
Close files properly

questions.question: Explain all file open modes in C++ with examples of when to use each mode.
keyPoints: ios::in - for reading (ifstream default)
ios::out - for writing (ofstream default)
ios::app - append mode (preserves data, writes at end)
ios::ate - at end (pointer at end initially but can seek)
ios::trunc - truncate (default for ofstream, deletes existing)
ios::binary - binary mode (no text translation)
Examples for each mode with code snippets

questions.question: Create a menu-driven C++ program to perform operations on a text file: create, append, display, search a word, and count characters/words/lines.
keyPoints: Use switch-case for menu
Create: open in ios::out mode
Append: open in ios::app mode
Display: open in ios::in mode, read line by line
Search: read word by word, compare using find()
Count: use counters for lines, words, characters
Use proper file open modes and error checking
Close files after operations
